query: PostgreSQL write bottleneck single node limitations high throughput ingestion 50000 events per second
answer: A single PostgreSQL node struggles to handle more than ~2,000 writes per second under realistic conditions. Traditional RDBMS architectures hit performance limits at high throughput. Tuning cannot overcome physical I/O limits.

results.title: PostgreSQL Write Performance: What the Benchmarks Won't Tell You
results.snippet: **TL;DR:** On a 16-core Ryzen 9 with a top-tier NVMe and 62 GB RAM, a single PostgreSQL 18 node tops out at **~1,875 realistic transactional writes per second** with full durability. PostgreSQL's own documentation explicitly states this "does not create any risk of database inconsistency." Using `synchronous_commit=off` per-transaction for non-critical writes (logging, metrics, notifications) is a legitimate, documented production pattern. **The realistic write ceiling on strong hardware is ~2,000 rps.** On a Ryzen 9 7950X with a top-tier NVMe drive and 62 GB of RAM (hardware better than most production database servers), the ceiling for a standard multi-statement OLTP write with 5 indexes, production timeouts, and full durability is approximately 1,875 writes per second. The gap between "1 million inserts/sec" demos and ~1,875 realistic transactional writes is almost entirely explained by three things: WAL fsync, row-level locks under contention, and the cost of full ACID guarantees that most synthetic benchmarks quietly disable.

results.title: The Database Bottleneck: Why Your RDBMS Will Fail at 500k Writes/Sec (And What to Build Instead)
results.snippet: # The Database Bottleneck: Why Your RDBMS Will Fail at 500k Writes/Sec (And What to Build Instead) | by The Speed Engineer | Beyond Localhost | Medium. ## Beyond Localhost. # The Database Bottleneck: Why Your RDBMS Will Fail at 500k Writes/Sec (And What to Build Instead). ## At extreme write velocity, traditional databases don’t degrade gracefully — they hit a performance cliff and stall completely. Image 3: The Speed Engineer. _Traditional database architectures don’t gracefully degrade under extreme write loads — they hit a saturation point where latency spikes exponentially before everything stalls. The physical limits of random disk I/O create hard ceilings that no amount of tuning can overcome._. But we’d crossed 480,000 writes per second and everything stopped. You can’t buy your way out of a broken design. ## Create an account to read the full story. If you’re new to Medium, create a new account to read this story on us. ## Published in Beyond Localhost.
